title: Getting Started
description: Install and set up CtroEnv in your TypeScript project with zero dependencies.
section: Install and set up CtroEnv in your TypeScript project with zero dependencies.
content:
# Getting Started

CtroEnv is a TypeScript-first environment variable management toolkit. Define your schema once, get
full type inference, validation, and error messages — with zero runtime dependencies.

title: Getting Started
description: Install and set up CtroEnv in your TypeScript project with zero dependencies.
content:
Install the core package:

For CLI tooling:

For framework-specific adapters:

title: Getting Started
description: Install and set up CtroEnv in your TypeScript project with zero dependencies.
content:
Define a schema using validator functions (string(), number(), boolean(), pick())
Chain refinements like .url(), .min(), .default() to add constraints
Call defineEnv() to validate against the environment source
Use the returned object — TypeScript infers the exact shape

If any variable is missing or invalid, defineEnv() throws a CtroEnvError with all errors
collected and formatted.

title: Getting Started
description: Install and set up CtroEnv in your TypeScript project with zero dependencies.
content:
Quick Start — build your first schema
Core Concepts — understand validators and schemas
string() — string validation reference
API Reference — full defineEnv documentation

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
section: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
# Core Concepts

CtroEnv is built around three core concepts: validators, schemas, and defineEnv().

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
A validator is a function that takes an input value and either returns a parsed value or
collects errors. Each validator has a specific type and can be refined with chainable methods.

CtroEnv provides four built-in validators:

| Validator | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| string() | string | Accepts string values |
| number() | number | Accepts numbers and numeric strings (coerces) |
| boolean() | boolean | Accepts booleans, "true"/"false", "1"/"0" (coerces) |
| pick([...]) | "a" \| "b" \| ... | Accepts only specific string values |

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
string(): Only accepts actual strings. Non-strings produce a type error.
number(): Accepts number values and numeric strings like "42". Rejects NaN,
  Infinity, empty strings, and non-numeric strings.
boolean(): Accepts true, false, numbers 1/0, and strings "true"/"false"/
  "yes"/"no"/"on"/"off"/"1"/"0" (case-insensitive, trimmed).
pick(): Only accepts exact string matches from the provided list.

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
Refinements add constraints on top of a validator. They are chainable:

Each refinement returns a new validator with the additional constraint — the original
validator is not mutated. This means you can reuse base validators:

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
These methods are available on every validator:

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| .optional() | Marks the variable as optional (value is undefined when missing) |
| .default(value) | Sets a default value when the variable is missing |
| .describe(text) | Attaches a human-readable description |
| .secret() | Marks as secret (masked in CLI output and logs) |
| .validate(fn) | Adds a custom validation function |

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
A schema is a plain object where keys are environment variable names and values are validators:

The schema definition is a Record>. TypeScript infers the
output type automatically.

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
defineEnv() is the main entry point. It takes a schema and optional source, then:
Walks each key in the schema
Reads the value from the environment source
Runs the validator
Collects all errors (doesn't throw on first error)
Returns a typed Proxy object — or throws CtroEnvError with all errors

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
An environment source is any object with a get(key: string) => string | undefined method.

| Source | Package | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Default (detectSource) | @ctroenv/core | Checks import.meta.env first, falls back to process.env |
| loadEnv() | @ctroenv/node | Reads from .env files |
| nodeSource() | @ctroenv/node | Wraps process.env explicitly |
| viteSource() | @ctroenv/vite | Reads from import.meta.env |
| Plain object | any | Pass { KEY: "value" } directly |
| objectSource(obj) | @ctroenv/core | Wrap a plain object as an EnvSource |

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
When validation fails, defineEnv() throws a CtroEnvError:

Each ValidationError has:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| key | string | The environment variable name |
| message | string | Human-readable error message |
| code | ErrorCode | Machine-readable error code |
| value | unknown \| undefined | The original (invalid) value |
| suggestion | string \| undefined | Suggested fix |

title: Core Concepts
description: Understand how CtroEnv works: schemas, validators, parsers, sources, and the defineEnv API.
content:
| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| missingrequired | Variable is required but not set |
| typemismatch | Value has the wrong type |
| invalidvalue | Value doesn't pass a refinement |
| validationfailed | Custom .validate() function failed |

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
section: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content:
# defineEnv()

The core function that validates environment variables against a schema and returns a typed
environment object.

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content: A schema definition object where keys are environment variable names and values are validators:

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content:
| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| source | EnvSource \| Record | Auto-detected | The environment source to read from |
| prefix | string | undefined | Prefix added to each key when looking up in the source |

#### source

Controls where environment variables are read from. If omitted, detectSource() checks
import.meta.env first, then falls back to process.env.

#### prefix

When set, all key lookups are prefixed. For example, with prefix: "MYAPP",
DATABASEURL in the schema looks up MYAPPDATABASEURL in the source.

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content:
Returns a read-only EnvResult object. TypeScript infers the type from the schema:

The object is always wrapped in a Proxy that:
Masks secret value reads with "********"
Exposes raw values via env.meta.get("KEY")
Prevents mutation (set, delete)
Masks secrets in JSON.stringify()

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content:
Required (default): The value is guaranteed to exist. Type is T (non-nullable).
.optional(): Type is T | undefined.
.default(value): Type is T (non-nullable), falls back to the default when missing.

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content:
Throws CtroEnvError if one or more variables are missing or invalid:

See Error Handling for details on all error types.

title: defineEnv()
description: The main entry point for creating a type-safe environment variable schema.
content:
defineEnv() uses TypeScript's type system to infer the exact shape of the returned object:
string() → string
number() → number
boolean() → boolean
pick(["a", "b"]) → "a" | "b"
.optional() → adds | undefined
.default(v) → removes | undefined (value always exists)

The inferred type is correctly reflected in IDE autocomplete and type checking.

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
section: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content:
# string()

Creates a validator that accepts string values.

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content:
Only actual string values pass the base validator. Non-strings produce a type error with a
suggestion to wrap the value in quotes.

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content: String refinements add additional constraints. Chain .min(), .max(), .url(), .email(), .port(), .regex() before .secret(), .optional(), .describe(). Reason: .secret() and .optional() return a generic wrapper that drops type-specific methods like .url().

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content: Requires the value to be a valid URL (parsed via new URL()):

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content:
Requires the value to match a basic email pattern:

Uses the regex: /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content: Requires the value to be a valid port number string (1-65535):

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content: Requires the string to be at least length characters:

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content: Requires the string to be at most length characters:

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content: Requires the string to match a regular expression:

title: string()
description: Create a string validator with URL, email, regex, min, max, and secret checks.
content:
All refinement methods return a new StringValidator, enabling chaining:

The original validator is never mutated.

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
section: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content:
# number()

Creates a validator that accepts number values. Also accepts numeric strings (coerces to number).

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content:
| Input | Result | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 42 | ✅ 42 | Actual number |
| "42" | ✅ 42 | Numeric string, coerced |
| "3.14" | ✅ 3.14 | Float string, coerced |
| "" | ❌ Type error | Empty string |
| "abc" | ❌ Type error | Non-numeric |
| NaN | ❌ Invalid | Non-finite |
| Infinity | ❌ Invalid | Non-finite |
| true | ❌ Type error | Boolean |
| null | ❌ Type error | Not a number |

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content: Requires the number to be an integer:

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content: Requires the number to be greater than zero:

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content: Requires the number to be a valid port (1-65535, integer):

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content: Requires the number to be greater than or equal to value:

title: number()
description: Create a number validator with port, min, max, integer, and float checks.
content: Requires the number to be less than or equal to value:

title: boolean()
description: Create a boolean validator that parses true/false string values.
section: Create a boolean validator that parses true/false string values.
content:
# boolean()

Creates a validator that accepts boolean values. Supports string and numeric coercion.

title: boolean()
description: Create a boolean validator that parses true/false string values.
content:
| Input | Result | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| true | ✅ true | Actual boolean |
| false | ✅ false | Actual boolean |
| "true" | ✅ true | Case-insensitive, trimmed |
| "false" | ✅ false | Case-insensitive, trimmed |
| "TRUE" | ✅ true | Case-insensitive |
| "1" | ✅ true | String "1" |
| "0" | ✅ false | String "0" |
| "yes" | ✅ true | Case-insensitive |
| "no" | ✅ false | Case-insensitive |
| "on" | ✅ true | Case-insensitive |
| "off" | ✅ false | Case-insensitive |
| 1 | ✅ true | Number 1 |
| 0 | ✅ false | Number 0 |
| 2 | ❌ Type error | Only 1 and 0 are accepted |
| null | ❌ Type error | |

title: boolean()
description: Create a boolean validator that parses true/false string values.
content:
boolean() has no type-specific refinements. It only has the
chainable methods (.optional(), .default(), .describe(),
.secret(), .validate()).

title: pick()
description: Create an enum validator that restricts values to a specific set of allowed options.
section: Create an enum validator that restricts values to a specific set of allowed options.
content:
# pick()

Creates a validator that accepts only specific string values (like an enum).

title: pick()
description: Create an enum validator that restricts values to a specific set of allowed options.
content:
| Param | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| values | readonly string[] | Array of allowed string values |

title: pick()
description: Create an enum validator that restricts values to a specific set of allowed options.
content: Only exact string matches from the provided list:

title: pick()
description: Create an enum validator that restricts values to a specific set of allowed options.
content:
When the input doesn't match any value, pick() compares case-insensitively and by prefix
to suggest the closest match:

title: pick()
description: Create an enum validator that restricts values to a specific set of allowed options.
content:
The TypeScript type is inferred as a union of the supplied literals:

This enables exhaustive checking:

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
section: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
# Chainable Methods

Every validator provides a set of common methods for controlling behavior, metadata, and
value handling. These are available on all validators: string(), number(), boolean(),
and pick().

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
Marks the variable as optional. When the environment variable is not set, the value will be
undefined instead of causing a missing-required error.

TypeScript infers T | undefined, so you must check for undefined before using the value:

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
Provides a fallback value when the environment variable is not set. Overrides any
.optional() setting — the value is always present.

TypeScript infers T (non-nullable) because the default ensures the value always exists.

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
Attaches a human-readable description to the validator. This description appears in:
Error messages: "Missing required environment variable: DATABASE_URL -- Primary database connection"
CLI output: Shown in the ctroenv docs command output
Generated .env.example files: Displayed as comments

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
Marks the variable as sensitive. Secret values are:
Masked at runtime: Reading env.JWTSECRET returns "**" instead of the real value
Masked in CLI output: Shown as •••••••• instead of the actual value
Masked in errors: Error messages never contain the raw secret value
Masked in serialization: JSON.stringify(env) replaces secrets with "**"
Masked in generated docs**: The value is excluded from .env.example content

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
To retrieve the actual secret value, use the .meta API:

The meta object is non-enumerable — it won't appear in Object.keys(), for...in, or JSON.stringify(). Access it explicitly when you need the raw value.

title: Chainable Methods
description: Common chainable methods shared across all validators: optional, default, describe.
content:
Adds a custom validation function. The function receives the parsed value and a context
object. Return undefined to pass, or a string error message to fail.

The context provides:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| key | string | The environment variable name |
| path | readonly string[] | The full path to the value (for nested schemas) |

This enables conditional validation based on other values:

However, validators cannot reference env during definition (it doesn't exist yet).
For cross-field validation, use a wrapper function:

Note: The validate function runs after type checking and other refinements. The value
is guaranteed to be of the correct type.

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
section: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content:
# Refinements

Refinements are standalone functions that wrap an existing validator to add a constraint.
They are the building blocks used internally by validator methods like .url() and .min().

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content:
These functions are exported from @ctroenv/core for cases where you want to apply a
refinement to a custom validator.

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content: Wraps a string validator to require a valid URL:

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content: Wraps a string validator to require a valid email format.

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content: Wraps a string validator to require a regex match.

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content:
Wraps a string or number validator to require a minimum length/value.
For strings: checks value.length >= limit
For numbers: checks value >= limit

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content:
Wraps a string or number validator to require a maximum length/value.
For strings: checks value.length <= limit
For numbers: checks value <= limit

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content: Wraps a number or string validator to require a valid port (1-65535).

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
content: Wraps a number validator to require an integer value.

title: Refinements
description: Apply standalone refinement functions like url(), email(), min(), max(), and more.
section: When to Use Refinements vs Methods
content:
In most cases, you should use the type-specific methods:

Standalone refinements are useful when building custom validator factories or when you
need to apply a refinement that isn't available as a method:

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
section: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content:
# Error Handling

CtroEnv provides a structured error system with machine-readable codes and human-readable
messages with suggestions.

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content:
When one or more validations fail, defineEnv() throws a CtroEnvError:

The error message is automatically formatted using formatErrors().

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content:
Each individual field error is a ValidationError object:

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| key | string | The environment variable name |
| message | string | Human-readable error description |
| code | ErrorCode | Machine-readable error category |
| value | unknown | The original value that failed validation |
| suggestion | string \| undefined | A suggested fix (when available) |

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content: A required environment variable was not found in the source.

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content: The value exists but has the wrong type (e.g., a string where a number is expected).

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content: The value has the correct type but fails a refinement (e.g., not a valid URL).

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content: A custom .validate() function returned an error message.

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content:
Produces a structured, colorized CLI output from an array of errors:

The output is grouped by error category ("Missing required" vs "Invalid") and includes
colorized ANSI output when the terminal supports it. Respects NO_COLOR, CI, and
TERM=dumb environment variables.

title: Error Handling
description: Handle validation errors with CtroEnvError, formatErrors, and error categories.
content:
The ErrorCode constant uses as const, making each value a literal type. Access the
union type via ErrorCodeType:

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
section: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content:
# Schema Composition

In monorepos, multiple packages often need different subsets of environment variables while sharing common ones. Schema composition lets you define shared contracts once and extend them per-service.

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content: Defines a reusable schema block. At runtime it's an identity function — it returns the same object. At type level it preserves exact validator types for extension.

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content: Merges a base schema with additional validators. Extension keys override base keys when they conflict.

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content:
When a key exists in both the base and extension, the extension wins:

In development mode (NODE_ENV === "development"), a warning is logged when conflicts occur.

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content: The recommended pattern for monorepos:

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content: Each service gets exactly the env vars it needs, sharing common validation logic through the base schema.

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content: Schemas can be chained to compose three or more layers:

title: Schema Composition
description: Share and extend environment variable schemas across packages with defineSchema and extendSchema.
content: Composed schemas maintain full type inference through defineEnv():

title: CLI Overview
description: The ctroenv CLI tool for validating, generating, and documenting environment variables.
section: The ctroenv CLI tool for validating, generating, and documenting environment variables.
content:
# CLI Overview

CtroEnv CLI provides command-line tools for validating, generating, checking, and documenting
environment variables.

title: CLI Overview
description: The ctroenv CLI tool for validating, generating, and documenting environment variables.
content: Or use directly with npx:

title: CLI Overview
description: The ctroenv CLI tool for validating, generating, and documenting environment variables.
content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| validate | Validate environment variables against a schema |
| generate | Generate .env.example from a schema |
| check | CI-friendly validation (exit codes, JSON output) |
| docs | Generate documentation from a schema |
| init | Scaffold a new CtroEnv project |

title: CLI Overview
description: The ctroenv CLI tool for validating, generating, and documenting environment variables.
content: Create a ctroenv.config.ts file in your project root:

title: CLI Overview
description: The ctroenv CLI tool for validating, generating, and documenting environment variables.
content: Pass the --help flag to any command for detailed options:

title: ctroenv validate
description: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
section: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
content:
# ctroenv validate

Validate environment variables against your schema.

title: ctroenv validate
description: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
content:
| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| --source | string | process.env | Path to a .env file |
| --watch | boolean | false | Watch for file changes |
| --json | boolean | false | Output as JSON |

title: ctroenv validate
description: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
content: Validates against process.env using the schema from ctroenv.config.ts.

title: ctroenv validate
description: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
content: Re-validates when your schema or env files change. Useful during development.

title: ctroenv validate
description: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
content: Outputs validation results as JSON for programmatic consumption:

title: ctroenv validate
description: Validate current environment variables against your schema from the CLI.
content:
| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 0 | All variables valid |
| 1 | Validation errors found |
| 2 | Configuration error |

title: ctroenv generate
description: Generate .env.example and TypeScript type declarations from your schema.
section: Generate .env.example and TypeScript type declarations from your schema.
content:
# ctroenv generate

Generate a .env.example file from your schema.

title: ctroenv generate
description: Generate .env.example and TypeScript type declarations from your schema.
content:
| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| --output | string | .env.example | Output file path |
| --no-comments | boolean | false | Omit comments from output |

title: ctroenv generate
description: Generate .env.example and TypeScript type declarations from your schema.
content:
The generated file includes:
Comments describing each variable (from .describe() in your schema)
Type and required/optional annotations
Default values when applicable
Secret variables are left empty (value not written)

title: ctroenv check
description: CI-friendly command that validates env vars and exits with the appropriate code.
section: CI-friendly command that validates env vars and exits with the appropriate code.
content:
# ctroenv check

CI-friendly validation command. Designed for continuous integration pipelines.

title: ctroenv check
description: CI-friendly command that validates env vars and exits with the appropriate code.
content:
| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| --source | string | .env | Path to the env file to check |
| --strict | boolean | false | Validate values against schema (not just keys) |
| --json | boolean | false | Output as JSON |

title: ctroenv check
description: CI-friendly command that validates env vars and exits with the appropriate code.
content:
| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 0 | All variables valid |
| 1 | Validation errors found |
| 2 | Configuration error |

Combine with .env.example in CI:

title: ctroenv check
description: CI-friendly command that validates env vars and exits with the appropriate code.
content:
Without --strict, check only compares key sets — it reports missing and
unused keys but does not validate values.

With --strict, each value is validated against its schema validator (same as
ctroenv validate). This catches type mismatches, invalid URLs, out-of-range
numbers, and other value-level errors.

# CLI Configuration

Configure the CLI behavior with a config file in your project root.

|---|---|---|
| TypeScript | ctroenv.config.ts | Full autocomplete with defineConfig() |
| JavaScript | ctroenv.config.js | JSDoc hints via @type |
| JSON | ctroenv.json | Simple, no imports needed |

The CLI auto-detects which file to load. If multiple exist, the first found
in the order above is used.

returns the config object.

Use ctroenv init --json to generate one.

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.

content:
# Node Adapter

The @ctroenv/node package provides .env file loading and process.env access for
Node.js applications.

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content: Loads environment variables from .env files in a priority chain.

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content:
| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| path | string | process.cwd() | Root directory to search for .env files |
| encoding | BufferEncoding | "utf-8" | File encoding |
| override | boolean | false | If false, process.env takes priority |
| system | boolean | false | Fall back to process.env when key not in files |

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content:
Files are loaded in order, with later files overriding earlier ones:
.env
.env.{NODE_ENV} (e.g., .env.development)
.env.local

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content: By default, process.env takes priority over file values:

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content:
A simpler alternative that only wraps process.env:

Unlike the default detectSource() (which checks import.meta.env first, then
falls back to process.env), nodeSource() only reads from process.env. Use
it when you want to skip the import.meta.env check.

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content: Parses a .env file string into key-value pairs. Supports the full .env file syntax.

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content:
| Feature | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Comments | KEY=val # comment | { KEY: "val" } |
| export prefix | export KEY=val | { KEY: "val" } |
| Quoted values | KEY="hello world" | { KEY: "hello world" } |
| Multiline (trailing \) | KEY=line one\line two | { KEY: "line one\nline two" } |
| Variable interpolation | URL=$BASE/api or URL=\${BASE}/api | Resolved from earlier keys, then process.env |
| Escaped $ with $$ | KEY=price$$10 | { KEY: "price$10" } |
| Escaped quotes | KEY=it's fine or KEY="say \"hi\"" | Preserves literal quotes |

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content:
Variables are resolved from previously parsed keys in the same file, then
falls back to process.env. Forward references are not supported —
variables must be defined before they are used.

title: Node Adapter
description: Load .env files in Node.js with loadEnv and nodeSource for CtroEnv schemas.
content: The EnvSource type is re-exported for convenience:

Creates an EnvSource that first reads from Vite's import.meta.env, then falls back

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
section: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content:
# Next.js Adapter

The @ctroenv/nextjs package provides Next.js integration with client/server environment
variable splitting and build-time validation.

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content:
Next.js requires splitting environment variables into server and client schemas:
Server variables: Only accessible on the server. Accessing from the browser throws
  an error.
Client variables: Must be prefixed with NEXTPUBLIC. Accessible everywhere.

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content: Returns a proxied environment object that enforces server/client boundaries:

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content: On the server, both server and client schemas are validated against process.env.

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content:
In the browser, only the client schema is resolved. Server variables return empty
values, and accessing them throws a descriptive error:

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content:
Validates environment variables at config load time, before your app starts building:

Validation runs eagerly when the config is loaded, so it works with any bundler — webpack,
Turbopack, or whatever comes next. If validation fails, errors are logged and the build
exits with code 1.

Composes with any existing webpack function in your config.

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content: The InferredNextEnv type automatically infers the shape from your schema:

title: Next.js Adapter
description: Integrate CtroEnv with Next.js for client/server env variable separation.
content:
Always validate server-side: Use withCtroEnv() in next.config.ts to catch
   issues during build.
Prefix client vars: All client-facing variables must start with NEXTPUBLIC.
Don't expose secrets: Server-only variables are completely inaccessible from
   the browser — no risk of accidental exposure.
Type safety: The proxied object provides full TypeScript autocomplete for both
   server and client variables.

title: Migration from t3-env
description: Migrate from t3-env to CtroEnv with this side-by-side comparison guide.
section: Migrate from t3-env to CtroEnv with this side-by-side comparison guide.
content:
# Migration from t3-env

CtroEnv is heavily inspired by t3-env and shares the same chainable validator pattern.
Migration is straightforward.

title: Migration from t3-env
description: Migrate from t3-env to CtroEnv with this side-by-side comparison guide.
content:
| Feature | t3-env | CtroEnv |
|---|---|---|
| Runtime deps | zod (required) | Zero dependencies |
| Framework adapters | Built-in | Separate packages (@ctroenv/node, @ctroenv/vite, @ctroenv/nextjs) |
| CLI | None | Full CLI (validate, generate, check, docs, init) |
| Error messages | Basic | Rich, grouped, with suggestions |
| Secret masking | Not supported | .secret() method |
| Type inference | Via zod inference | Native inference |
| Bundle size | ~50KB+ (zod included) | under 5KB gzipped |
| Validation | Throws on first error | Collects all errors |

title: Migration from t3-env
description: Migrate from t3-env to CtroEnv with this side-by-side comparison guide.
content: t3-env throws on the first error. CtroEnv collects ALL errors:

title: Migration from t3-env
description: Migrate from t3-env to CtroEnv with this side-by-side comparison guide.
content:
t3-env uses z.coerce.number() for string coercion. CtroEnv's number() handles
coercion automatically:

title: Migration from envalid

# Migration from envalid

If you're using envalid, migrating to CtroEnv gives you TypeScript-native inference and
a modern chainable API.

| Feature | envalid | CtroEnv |

| TypeScript inference | Manual type creation | Automatic inference |
| Error messages | Basic | Rich, grouped, with suggestions |
| CLI | None | Full CLI tooling |
| Framework adapters | None | Node, Vite, Next.js |
| Secret masking | Not supported | .secret() method |

title: Migration from envalid

content:
| envalid | CtroEnv |
|---|---|
| str() | string() |
| num() | number() |
| bool() | boolean() |
| url() | string().url() |
| email() | string().email() |
| json() | Custom .validate() |
| makeValidator() | .validate() |
| { default: v } | .default(v) |
| { choices: [...] } | pick([...]) |
| cleanEnv() | defineEnv() |

title: Migration from envalid
description: Migrate from envalid to CtroEnv with this side-by-side comparison guide.

section: Migrate from plain dotenv to CtroEnv for type safety and validation.
content:
# Migration from dotenv

If you're using dotenv with raw process.env access, CtroEnv adds validation,

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
section: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content:
# Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility

When CtroEnv runs in the browser — inside a Sanity Studio configuration, a client component, or any client-bundled code — it needs to access NEXTPUBLIC environment variables that Next.js provides at runtime. But how those variables are exposed depends on your bundler.
author: Ctrotech

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content:
In Next.js with webpack, NEXTPUBLIC values are inlined at compile time via DefinePlugin. Static access like process.env.NEXTPUBLICX gets replaced with the literal value. But dynamic access — process.env["NEXTPUBLICX"] — is not replaced, because the bundler can't statically analyze bracket notation.

CtroEnv's detectSource() function creates an EnvSource that looks up values dynamically:

On the server this is fine — process.env has everything. But in a Next.js client bundle with webpack, process.env is polyfilled but process.env["NEXTPUBLICX"] returns undefined. The polyfill object exists, but the values were only embedded for static property access patterns.
author: Ctrotech

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content:
Next.js 16 defaults to Turbopack, which handles environment variables differently. Turbopack provides import.meta.env as a runtime object — an actual JavaScript Object with all NEXTPUBLIC values set on it. Dynamic access via import.meta.env["NEXTPUBLICX"] works because the values are real properties on a real object.

But detectSource() checked process.env first, and on the client process is polyfilled (truthy check passes), so it returned the process.env source. Dynamic access failed. import.meta.env was never reached.
author: Ctrotech

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content:
detectSource() now returns a composite source that tries import.meta.env first, then falls back to process.env:

This handles all three environments:

| Environment | Primary Source | Fallback |
|---|---|---|
| Node.js server | — | process.env (no import.meta.env) |
| Turbopack client | import.meta.env (dynamic access works) | process.env |
| Webpack client | — | process.env (dynamic access still fails — needs explicit source) |

For webpack-based Next.js projects, users should pass an explicit source with statically-accessed values:
author: Ctrotech

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content: The @ctroenv/nextjs adapter's defineEnv() previously hardcoded { get: (key) => process.env[key] } as its source. It now uses detectSource() from @ctroenv/core, so both adapters benefit from the composite source behavior.
author: Ctrotech

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content: This fix primarily affects projects using CtroEnv with client-bundled code — Sanity Studio configurations, client component env validation, or any defineEnv() call that executes in the browser. If you're using Turbopack (Next.js 15+ default), the fix is seamless. Webpack users need the explicit source workaround.
author: Ctrotech

title: Composite Source Detection — Turbopack and Client-Side Compatibility
description: How detectSource() now prefers import.meta.env for dynamic NEXT_PUBLIC access in Next.js 16 Turbopack client bundles.
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
section: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content:
# Environment Variables in Monorepos

Monorepos create a tension: shared infrastructure needs shared env vars, but each package needs its own overrides. CtroEnv's schema composition gives you both.
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content:
In a typical monorepo, different packages need different env vars:
A database package needs DATABASEURL and DBPOOLSIZE
An API package needs PORT, CORSORIGIN, and the database vars
A worker package needs QUEUEURL and JWTSECRET
A shared config package might define NODEENV and LOGLEVEL for everyone

Without schema composition, you either duplicate definitions across packages or create a single monolithic schema that every package depends on — even when it shouldn't.
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content:
defineSchema() and extendSchema() let you compose schemas like building blocks:

Each package only imports the schema it needs. The API package inherits the database vars without redefining them.
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content:
Each package validates its own env at startup:

If the database team adds a DB_SSL field, the API package picks it up automatically when it rebuilds — no manual synchronization needed.
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content: Validate the entire monorepo in CI:
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content:
Schema composition also works when consuming external packages. If a library exposes its schema, extend it:

Dev-mode warns on key conflicts, so you know immediately if two schemas define the same variable with different validators.
author: Ctrotech

title: Environment Variables in Monorepos
description: Schema composition for reusable env definitions across monorepo packages with extendSchema.
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
section: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content:
# Protecting Secrets at Runtime

Accidentally logging a JWTSECRET or printing a DATABASEURL to the console is one of those mistakes that feels obvious in hindsight. CtroEnv's secret masking prevents the leak before it happens.
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content:
Standard practice says "keep secrets out of logs" — but in practice, they slip through:

These patterns are common in debugging sessions, error reports, and startup logs. One moment of carelessness and your production secret is in your log aggregator.
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content:
Mark a variable with .secret():

Any direct access to a secret key returns "********":

This works via a runtime Proxy. The env object intercepts property reads and masks any key that was defined with .secret(). Non-secret values pass through normally.
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content:
When you genuinely need the raw value, use env.meta:

The get() method is the explicit escape hatch. It signals intent — anyone reading the code can see "this line accesses a secret."
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content:
Secret masking integrates with serialization:

If you need the full object with real values, use:
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content: Errors also redact secrets. If a validation fails, the error message shows the key but not the attempted value — so you can debug without exposing credentials in stack traces.
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content:
Good candidates for .secret():
JWT secrets and signing keys
API tokens for external services
Database connection strings (if they contain credentials)
Encryption keys
OAuth client secrets

Not every env var needs masking. Non-sensitive values like PORT, NODEENV, and LOGLEVEL are fine as-is.
author: Ctrotech

title: Protecting Secrets at Runtime
description: Runtime secret masking prevents accidental leaks of JWT secrets, API keys, and database credentials.
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: Turbopack Compat, Better Types, and Quality Improvements
description: Eager validation for Turbopack, const type parameter for pick(), and string | undefined for env.meta.get().
section: Eager validation for Turbopack, const type parameter for pick(), and string | undefined for env.meta.get().
content:
# Turbopack Compat, Better Types, and Quality Improvements

Three fixes that make CtroEnv work seamlessly with modern tooling and feel more polished.
author: Ctrotech

title: Turbopack Compat, Better Types, and Quality Improvements
description: Eager validation for Turbopack, const type parameter for pick(), and string | undefined for env.meta.get().
content:
The withCtroEnv() Next.js wrapper previously ran validation inside a webpack config hook. With Next.js 16 defaulting to Turbopack, that validation was silently skipped.

Validation now runs eagerly at config load time — before any bundler touches your code:

Errors are caught immediately when the config is loaded, not buried inside a build step. The behavior is identical — failed validation still exits with code 1 — but it now works regardless of which bundler Next.js uses.
author: Ctrotech

title: Turbopack Compat, Better Types, and Quality Improvements
description: Eager validation for Turbopack, const type parameter for pick(), and string | undefined for env.meta.get().
content:
pick() now uses a const type parameter, eliminating the need for as const on every call:

The inferred type is "dev" | "prod" | "test" in both cases. One less thing to remember.
author: Ctrotech

title: Turbopack Compat, Better Types, and Quality Improvements
description: Eager validation for Turbopack, const type parameter for pick(), and string | undefined for env.meta.get().
content:
env.meta.get("KEY") now returns string | undefined instead of unknown:

Since env values are always strings at runtime, the return type now reflects reality. The undefined case handles missing keys, matching standard JavaScript access patterns.
author: Ctrotech

title: Turbopack Compat, Better Types, and Quality Improvements
description: Eager validation for Turbopack, const type parameter for pick(), and string | undefined for env.meta.get().
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.0 — Type-Safe Environment Variables for TypeScript
description: Initial release of CtroEnv: define, validate, and infer types for environment variables with zero runtime dependencies.
section: Initial release of CtroEnv: define, validate, and infer types for environment variables with zero runtime dependencies.
content:
# v1.0.0 — Type-Safe Environment Variables for TypeScript

CtroEnv v1.0.0 is live — a TypeScript-first environment variable management toolkit with zero runtime dependencies.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.0 — Type-Safe Environment Variables for TypeScript
description: Initial release of CtroEnv: define, validate, and infer types for environment variables with zero runtime dependencies.
content: Define, validate, and infer types for environment variables. 4 KB gzipped, zero dependencies, full TypeScript inference, no code generation step.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.0 — Type-Safe Environment Variables for TypeScript
description: Initial release of CtroEnv: define, validate, and infer types for environment variables with zero runtime dependencies.
content:
Existing solutions have tradeoffs:
dotenv: Loads .env files but provides no validation or type safety
t3-env: Great pattern but requires Zod (50 KB+ dependency)
envalid: Solid but lacks TypeScript-native inference

CtroEnv is built from scratch with zero dependencies, native TypeScript inference, and errors grouped by category with actionable suggestions.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.0 — Type-Safe Environment Variables for TypeScript
description: Initial release of CtroEnv: define, validate, and infer types for environment variables with zero runtime dependencies.
content:
| Package | Description |
|---|---|
| @ctroenv/core | Schema engine with validators and type inference |
| @ctroenv/shared | Internal shared utilities |
| @ctroenv/cli | CLI tooling (validate, generate, check, docs, init) |
| @ctroenv/node | Node.js .env file loading |
| @ctroenv/vite | Vite build-time validation plugin |
| @ctroenv/nextjs | Next.js client/server env splitting |
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.0 — Type-Safe Environment Variables for TypeScript
description: Initial release of CtroEnv: define, validate, and infer types for environment variables with zero runtime dependencies.
content:
Documentation: ctroenv.vercel.app
GitHub: github.com/ctrotech-tutor/ctroenv
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience
description: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
section: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
content:
# v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience

This patch release focuses on error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and
bundle size optimization.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience
description: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
content:
Error messages have been completely overhauled:
Centralized factory: Consistent error creation across all validators
Richer formatting: Errors are grouped by category (Missing vs Invalid) with colored hints
Actionable suggestions: Every error includes a suggested fix
NOCOLOR support: Respects NOCOLOR, CI, and TERM=dumb environment variables
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience
description: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
content:
Core validation benchmarks:

| Scenario | Ops/sec |
|---|---|
| 5 fields (pass) | ~253,000 |
| 5 fields (fail) | ~10,600 |
| 20 fields (pass) | ~69,000 |
| CLI parse (3 fields) | ~507,000 |
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience
description: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
content:
Zero dependency core remains tiny:

| Package | Size (gzipped) |
|---|---|
| @ctroenv/core | 4.05 KB |
| @ctroenv/cli (binary) | 4 KB |
| @ctroenv/node | 508 B |
| @ctroenv/vite | 458 B |
| @ctroenv/nextjs | 628 B |
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience
description: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
content:
refine() now preserves type-specific methods via Object.assign, so chains like
number().min(0).max(100) work correctly.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.0.1 — Polish & Developer Experience
description: Error message refinement, performance benchmarks, and bundle size optimization.
content:
See the GitHub Release
for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.0 — Secret Masking & Schema Composition
description: Runtime secret masking, schema composition for monorepos, and universal AI agent guide.
section: Runtime secret masking, schema composition for monorepos, and universal AI agent guide.
content:
# v1.1.0 — Secret Masking & Schema Composition

Runtime secret masking that prevents accidental leaks, and schema composition for monorepo-scale env management.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.0 — Secret Masking & Schema Composition
description: Runtime secret masking, schema composition for monorepos, and universal AI agent guide.
content:
Mark sensitive keys with .secret() — direct access returns "********", raw values require explicit intent via env.meta.get():

The env object uses a Proxy to mask values at runtime. Error messages redact secrets too.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.0 — Secret Masking & Schema Composition
description: Runtime secret masking, schema composition for monorepos, and universal AI agent guide.
content:
defineSchema() + extendSchema() for reusable schema blocks:

Extension keys override base. Dev-mode warns on conflicts.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.0 — Secret Masking & Schema Composition
description: Runtime secret masking, schema composition for monorepos, and universal AI agent guide.
content: CtroEnv now ships AGENTS.md at root — a universal AI agent guide read by opencode, Cursor, Copilot, Claude Code, and Cline.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.0 — Secret Masking & Schema Composition
description: Runtime secret masking, schema composition for monorepos, and universal AI agent guide.
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
section: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content:
# v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements

Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and more.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content: Previously, env.meta only existed when at least one variable was marked .secret(). Now env.meta is always present — the type wasn't lying, but the runtime was.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content: The server-only error was dead code on the client — it checked key in server but server was always {} on the client, so the error was never thrown and silently returned undefined. The proxy now receives the server key names directly.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content: All error output now uses process.stderr, keeping process.stdout clean for piping and JSON output.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content: The JavaScript template was using require()/module.exports but @ctroenv/cli is "type": "module". Templates now use import/export default.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content:
extendSchema() guards against non-Node runtimes
NOCOLOR="" disables colors per spec
Boolean validator accepts yes/no/on/off
--watch mode watches schema + source, not whole CWD
withCtroEnv() throws instead of process.exit(1)
Dead errCoerce / coercionfailed removed
Tagline removed from formatErrors() output
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.1.1 — Bug Fixes & Quality Improvements
description: Fixes for env.meta, Next.js server-only key guard, CLI stderr, and boolean validator polish.
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements
description: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
section: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
content:
# v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements

Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements
description: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
content:
@ctroenv/node's parseEnvFile() has been rewritten with full .env spec support:

New capabilities:
$VAR and ${VAR} interpolation — references earlier variables, falls back to process.env
$$ escaping — produces a literal $
Multiline values — trailing backslash continues on next line
Inline comments — KEY=value # comment strips the comment
export prefix — export KEY=value is parsed correctly
Escaped quotes — \" and \' inside quoted values
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements
description: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
content:
ctroenv.json is now a first-class config format alongside ctroenv.config.ts and ctroenv.config.js:

The schema loader also searches for env.json and env.config.json alongside the existing .ts/.js variants, giving you more flexibility in how you define your configuration.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements
description: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
content:
The check command now validates actual values against your schema:

Previous releases only reported missing or extra keys. Now every value is run through defineEnv() so you catch type mismatches, URL validation failures, and out-of-range numbers at check time.
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements
description: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
content:
validate --strict removed — was dead code since v1.0.0; use check --strict instead
Config wiring: sources.default now correctly feeds into check --source and validate --source defaults
Docs site: comprehensive audit with 13 fixes across documentation pages, READMEs, and skill files
author: Ctrotech

title: v1.2.0 — JSON Config, Env File Parsing, CLI Improvements
description: Smarter env file parsing, JSON configuration support, and check --strict for value validation.
content: See the GitHub Release for the complete list of changes.
author: Ctrotech

title: Why CtroEnv?
description: Bring the same rigor from your type system to the environment variables your app depends on.
section: Bring the same rigor from your type system to the environment variables your app depends on.
content:
# Why CtroEnv?

Most TypeScript projects treat environment variables as a weakly-typed free-for-all. CtroEnv brings the same rigor you expect from your type system to the variables your app depends on.
author: Ctrotech

title: Why CtroEnv?
description: Bring the same rigor from your type system to the environment variables your app depends on.
content:
Here's how most projects handle env vars:

This works, but it's scattered across your codebase. Every file repeats the same checks. Types are inferred as string | undefined — or worse, you assert with ! and hope for the best.

Types don't tell you what's expected. A linter won't catch a missing DATABASE_URL until runtime.
author: Ctrotech

title: Why CtroEnv?
description: Bring the same rigor from your type system to the environment variables your app depends on.
content:
Define everything in one place:

Every access is typed. Every value is validated at startup. Missing or invalid variables produce clear, actionable errors — grouped by category with suggested fixes.
author: Ctrotech

title: Why CtroEnv?
description: Bring the same rigor from your type system to the environment variables your app depends on.
content:
Zero dependencies. The core package is 4 KB gzipped with no runtime dependencies. No lodash, no zod, no io-ts.

Framework adapters. Node.js, Vite, and Next.js each get their own source adapter. The same schema works across all environments:

Secret masking. Mark a variable with .secret() and direct access returns "*****" — raw values require explicit intent.

Schema composition. defineSchema() + extendSchema() for reusable blocks across monorepo packages.

CLI tooling.** Validate, check, generate docs, and scaffold config — all from the command line.
author: Ctrotech

title: Why CtroEnv?
description: Bring the same rigor from your type system to the environment variables your app depends on.
content: Or check the quick start guide for a step-by-step walkthrough.
author: Ctrotech
